title: On Bennequin Type Inequality for Symplectic Caps of (S3,ξstd).
speaker: Anubhav Mukherjee
abstract: In this talk I will discuss a Bennequin type inequality for symplectic caps of S3 with standard contact structure. This has interesting applications which can help us to understand the smooth topology of symplectic caps and smoothly embedded suraces inside this. This is a joint work with Masaki Taniguchi and Nobuo Iida.
tags: Joint IAS/Princeton University Symplectic Geometry Seminar

proof_stages.label: From surface embeddings to H-sliceness in symplectic caps
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates the problem from smooth four-manifold topology, recalls that adjunction inequalities constrain surfaces in closed symplectic four-manifolds, defines symplectic fillings and caps, introduces H-sliceness of knots in a four-manifold with boundary, and reformulates puncturing a closed symplectic four-manifold as producing a symplectic cap.

proof_stages.label: Filling-side Bennequin obstruction and cap-side contrast
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews the generalized Bennequin inequality for Legendrian knots in symplectic fillings, explaining how positive Thurston-Bennequin-type data obstructs bounding disks in fillings, and contrasts this with the much less controlled topology of symplectic caps.

proof_stages.label: Cap Bennequin-type theorem and sharpness issue
proof_stages.summary: The main result is stated: if a knot in the standard contact S^3 has positive maximal Thurston-Bennequin number, then it is not H-slice in a symplectic cap with b_1=0 and b_2^+ congruent to 3 mod 4, while the b_2^+ congruent to 1 mod 4 range requires extra care and motivates a conjectural extension for b_2^+>1.

proof_stages.label: Relative Bauer-Furuta-type invariant input
proof_stages.summary: As the gauge-theoretic black box, the proof uses Iida's refinement of the Kronheimer-Mrowka/Bauer-Furuta contact-boundary invariant, especially a connected-sum formula with a symplectic filling and nonvanishing for closed symplectic four-manifolds when b_2^+ is 3 mod 4.

proof_stages.label: Contradiction setup by capping and Weinstein handle attachment
proof_stages.summary: Assuming an H-slice disk exists in the cap, the cap is closed by attaching a Darboux ball, while positivity of maximal Thurston-Bennequin number is used to attach a zero-framed Weinstein two-handle to another four-ball to produce a symplectic filling W.

proof_stages.label: Handle slide, nontrivial square-zero sphere, and invariant contradiction
proof_stages.summary: In the connected sum W#X, the H-trivial disk and the zero-framed handle traces are rearranged by a handle slide to produce a homologically nontrivial self-intersection-zero sphere, which forces the relative invariant to vanish, contradicting the nonvanishing from the symplectic connected-sum formula.

examples.name: Complex projective plane as the basic closed symplectic four-manifold
examples.note: The speaker uses CP^2 as a familiar simple closed symplectic four-manifold where adjunction-type ideas first appeared, only to orient the audience historically.
examples.engagement_reason: CP^2 is cited as a simple/basic closed symplectic four-manifold where earlier adjunction results were studied, without doing a computation or construction on it.

passes.reason: CP^2 is the standard homogeneous Kähler/symplectic four-manifold, used here as a basic background case rather than to refute a claim.

examples.name: Ordinary knot sliceness in the four-ball
examples.note: The case X = B^4 is used to make H-sliceness concrete, since the homological condition is then automatic and the definition reduces to the usual sliceness of a knot.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the H-sliceness definition to X=B^4 and checks that H_2 vanishes, so the homological condition is automatic and the notion reduces to ordinary sliceness.

passes.reason: The four-ball is the contractible base case where H_2 vanishes, so H-sliceness reduces to ordinary sliceness.

examples.name: Smooth H-sliceness in punctured connected sums
examples.note: The fact that every knot is H-slice in punctured connected sums of CP^2, reversed CP^2, or S^2 x S^2 is the smooth-topological benchmark that motivates asking what remains true under symplectic-cap restrictions.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explains how every knot becomes H-slice in punctured connected sums by using double band moves, adding CP^2/CPbar^2 or S^2×S^2 pieces, and canceling the resulting disks homologically.

passes.reason: Punctured connected sums of CP^2, its reverse, or S^2×S^2 are ordinary infinite-family smooth four-manifold examples used for comparison.
The punctured connected sums form a systematic smooth family illustrating that smooth H-sliceness is always possible.

examples.name: S^3 with unique filling but many caps
examples.note: The standard three-sphere illustrates the asymmetry between fillings and caps: its symplectic filling is unique up to blowups, while caps can have arbitrarily large topology.
examples.engagement_reason: S^3 is used as a comparison example with unique filling up to blowups but infinitely many/arbitrarily large caps, with no derivation of these facts.

passes.reason: The standard S^3 is a maximally regular contact boundary with the extremal feature of unique filling, used to show caps and fillings cannot simply be swapped.
Standard S^3 is the highly symmetric contact sphere with the extremal property of unique filling, used to refute cap/filling interchangeability.

Standard S^3 is used to refute the idea that caps and fillings can just be interchanged, since it has unique filling but infinitely many caps.
Used to refute the idea that caps and fillings can simply be swapped, since standard S^3 is maximally symmetric/simple and has the rigid extreme of unique filling but many caps.

Standard S^3 is maximally symmetric and fillability-rigid, and its unique filling versus many caps refutes the idea that fillings and caps can simply be swapped.

examples.name: Right-handed trefoil in E(1)
examples.note: The right-handed trefoil has maximal Thurston-Bennequin number +1 and is H-slice in the symplectic elliptic surface E(1) with b_2^+ = 1, forcing the theorem and conjecture to distinguish the b_2^+=1 case from larger b_2^+.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the right-handed trefoil in E(1) as a concrete test case, noting TB=+1 and b_2^+(E(1))=1 to contrast the theorem’s obstruction with its actual H-sliceness there.

passes.reason: The right-handed trefoil in the b_2^+=1 rational elliptic surface E(1) is a boundary-case example showing the proposed cap obstruction fails without the stated b_2^+ restriction.
The right-handed trefoil in E(1) lies in the b2+=1 boundary case and is H-slice despite positive TB, refuting extension of the theorem there.
The right-handed trefoil in E(1) lies on the b_2^+=1 boundary case and is H-slice there, so it refutes extending the stated obstruction to that case.
